title: Acute Epiglottitis in a 4-Year-Old (Incompletely Hib-Immunized) with the Sudden Onset of High Fever, Drooling, Dysphagia, a Muffled 'Hot-Potato' Voice, and Tripod Positioning: Protecting a Threatened Airway by NOT Examining the Throat and Keeping the Child Calm, While LEADING and COORDINATING the Emergency Airway Team (ENT + Anesthesia + OR), Communicating by SBAR, Delegating Roles Appropriately, and Escalating Through the Chain of Command
subtitle: ACUTE EPIGLOTTITIS (supraglottitis) = a rapidly progressive, LIFE-THREATENING bacterial infection causing inflammation/edema of the EPIGLOTTIS and supraglottic structures that can obstruct the airway within hours; classically caused by HAEMOPHILUS INFLUENZAE TYPE B (Hib) — now much rarer where Hib vaccination is routine, so current cases occur in UNDER-/UN-immunized children (as here) and are increasingly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, group A strep, and Staphylococcus aureus (and seen in older children/adults). It is a PEDIATRIC AIRWAY EMERGENCY. PRESENTATION (abrupt, over hours; toxic-appearing): high FEVER, the classic '4 Ds' — DROOLING (cannot/will not swallow secretions), DYSPHAGIA, DYSPHONIA (muffled, 'hot-potato' voice — NOT hoarse), and DISTRESS (anxious, air hunger, soft inspiratory stridor) — plus a TRIPOD / 'sniffing' position (sitting upright, leaning forward on hands, chin thrust, mouth open) to maximize the airway, restlessness/anxiety, and the ABSENCE of the harsh barky/seal-like cough that characterizes croup. KEY CONTRAST with CROUP (viral laryngotracheobronchitis): croup is GRADUAL onset, with a BARKY/SEAL cough + inspiratory stridor + hoarseness + low-grade fever in a non-toxic child, managed with nebulized epinephrine + dexamethasone + humidified air; EPIGLOTTITIS is ACUTE, TOXIC, drooling, muffled-voiced, with NO cough and IMPENDING airway obstruction → an emergency. THE CARDINAL RULE — DO NOT AGITATE THE CHILD OR EXAMINE THE THROAT: any agitation, crying, or instrumentation of the oropharynx can precipitate laryngospasm and COMPLETE, FATAL AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION. Therefore DO NOT: use a tongue depressor / inspect or swab the throat / obtain a throat culture / place an IV or draw blood / take a rectal temperature / force the child to lie down / separate the child from the parent — until a definitive airway is secured. KEEP the child CALM and UPRIGHT in a position of comfort (often on the parent's lap), minimize stimulation, provide blow-by humidified oxygen ONLY if tolerated without distress, and NEVER leave the child unattended. Lateral neck X-ray classically shows the 'THUMBPRINT SIGN' (swollen epiglottis) but imaging is obtained ONLY if the child is stable AND accompanied by airway-trained personnel — never send an unstable child to radiology alone, and never delay airway management for a film. DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT = SECURE THE AIRWAY in a CONTROLLED setting: the most experienced airway providers — typically ENT (otolaryngology) and ANESTHESIA — perform intubation in the OPERATING ROOM under inhalational anesthesia, with EMERGENCY TRACHEOSTOMY equipment immediately available in case intubation fails ('can't intubate, can't ventilate'). After the airway is secured: IV ANTIBIOTICS (e.g., ceftriaxone or cefotaxime, ± vancomycin for resistant organisms), humidified oxygen, PICU monitoring, and supportive care; corticosteroids are sometimes used to reduce edema. DROPLET PRECAUTIONS (Hib/respiratory spread). PREVENTION: the Hib VACCINE (this child missed doses); RIFAMPIN prophylaxis for unimmunized close/household contacts in confirmed Hib disease per public health. LEADERSHIP, DELEGATION, AND TEAM COORDINATION are central to survival in this emergency (and the focus of this case): the nurse must (1) RECOGNIZE the threatened airway immediately and assign the highest priority (AIRWAY first — ABCs); (2) ESCALATE without delay through the CHAIN OF COMMAND — notify the provider STAT and ACTIVATE the emergency airway team (ENT + anesthesia + a rapid-response/code resource) and alert the OR/PICU; (3) communicate using SBAR (Situation-Background-Assessment-Recommendation) for a concise, complete handoff to each responder; (4) use CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION and assign clear ROLES as the team arrives (who brings the difficult-airway/intubation cart + emergency tracheostomy tray + bag-valve-mask, who readies medications/IV access for AFTER the airway is secured, who continuously monitors the child, who supports and stays with the parent, who documents/records times); (5) DELEGATE APPROPRIATELY within scope — unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) can fetch equipment, bring the airway cart, obtain supplies, and help keep the environment calm, but the RN does NOT delegate the AIRWAY ASSESSMENT, ongoing monitoring of a deteriorating patient, or clinical judgment; the RN stays with the child and keeps them calm; (6) PRIORITIZE — defer all non-essential, potentially agitating tasks (IV, labs, throat exam) until the airway is controlled; (7) after the event, DEBRIEF the team. The overarching nursing aim: do nothing that could obstruct the airway, keep the child calm with the parent, and rapidly orchestrate the right people and equipment to definitively secure the airway

patient.name: L. Castillo
patient.code: Full code; parents are present + are decision-makers; goal = secure the airway safely and treat the infection while keeping the child calm and the parent at the bedside
patient.admitDate: Brought to the ED by his parents this evening with the ABRUPT onset (over ~6 hours) of high fever, refusal to eat or drink, DROOLING, a muffled voice, and rapidly worsening difficulty breathing. He is sitting bolt upright on his mother's lap, leaning forward (tripod), chin thrust forward, mouth open, drooling, anxious, with soft inspiratory stridor and no cough. Immunizations are INCOMPLETE (missed his Hib series). Triage immediately recognized a possible airway emergency, brought him + his mother straight back to a resuscitation room, avoided agitating him, did NOT examine his throat, and activated the team.
patient.room: ED resuscitation room; child upright on mother's lap, calm environment maintained; ENT + anesthesia + PICU + respiratory therapy activated; difficult-airway cart + emergency tracheostomy tray + bag-valve-mask at bedside; OR being readied for a controlled airway

vitals.rr: 34/min with soft inspiratory stridor + mild suprasternal retractions
vitals.spo2: 93% on room air (blow-by humidified O2 offered, tolerated)
vitals.temp: 39.4°C; TOXIC-appearing, anxious 4-year-old sitting upright/tripod on mother's lap, DROOLING, muffled 'hot-potato' voice, NO cough, refusing to swallow; kept calm + upright + with mother; throat NOT examined; airway team activated immediately

39.4°C; RN escalated via chain of command — provider at bedside, ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT paged STAT, OR notified; SBAR handoff given; difficult-airway cart + emergency tracheostomy tray + BVM brought to bedside; roles assigned (airway equipment, monitoring, parent support, documentation); IV access + labs + throat exam DEFERRED until airway secured; child remains calm on mother's lap

vitals.rr: intubated — controlled ventilation after successful intubation by anesthesia/ENT in the OR under inhalational anesthesia (cherry-red, swollen epiglottis visualized); emergency tracheostomy setup was on standby but not needed
vitals.temp: 39.0°C; airway SECURED in the OR by the most experienced providers; small ETT placed; emergency surgical-airway backup ready throughout; transferred to PICU; IV access now obtained + blood cultures drawn + IV antibiotics started

38.2°C (defervescing); IV ceftriaxone running (± vancomycin per protocol); blood cultures pending; droplet precautions in place; sedated + comfortable on the ventilator; parents updated + at bedside; airway edema being monitored for resolution before a planned extubation

37.2°C; epiglottic edema resolved (air-leak present + visualized improvement) → successfully EXTUBATED; breathing comfortably without stridor; tolerating oral fluids; continuing IV antibiotics; calmer, interactive, reunited fully with parents

36.9°C; afebrile + breathing normally + eating/drinking + playful; transitioning to oral antibiotics to complete the course; CATCH-UP IMMUNIZATIONS (incl. Hib) arranged; household-contact prophylaxis addressed with public health if Hib confirmed; discharge teaching + follow-up scheduled

labs.v: 4 y/o boy L. Castillo brought to the ED with the ABRUPT onset (~6 hours) of high fever, refusal to eat/drink, DROOLING, a muffled voice, and rapidly worsening breathing. On arrival he is TOXIC-appearing and anxious, sitting bolt upright in a TRIPOD position on his mother's lap (leaning forward on his hands, chin thrust forward, mouth open), DROOLING saliva, with a muffled 'HOT-POTATO' voice, soft inspiratory STRIDOR, mild retractions, and NO cough. The constellation — acute onset + high fever + the '4 Ds' (Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia, Distress) + tripod positioning + toxic appearance + absence of a barky cough — is classic for ACUTE EPIGLOTTITIS, a life-threatening airway emergency. IMMUNIZATIONS are INCOMPLETE: he missed his Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) series (the family had a gap in care) — relevant because Hib is the classic cause and under-immunization restores susceptibility (other causes now include S. pneumoniae, group A strep, S. aureus). PMH otherwise unremarkable; no prior intubation/airway anomaly. SOCIAL: lives with both parents + an infant sibling; mother present + appropriately frightened; Spanish-English bilingual home (professional interpreter available); the parents are cooperative and able to help keep him calm. CRITICAL DIFFERENTIATION from CROUP (viral laryngotracheobronchitis): CROUP is GRADUAL, with a harsh BARKY/SEAL-like cough, hoarseness, inspiratory stridor, low-grade fever, in a NON-toxic child, and is treated with nebulized epinephrine + dexamethasone + humidified air; EPIGLOTTITIS is ACUTE, TOXIC, DROOLING, muffled-voiced, with NO cough and IMPENDING airway obstruction requiring emergency airway management. Triage correctly recognized the airway threat, took the child + mother immediately to a resuscitation room, kept him calm and upright, did NOT attempt to examine the throat, and activated the team — the nurse's recognition + immediate escalation are pivotal.
labs.r: Acute epiglottitis = airway emergency: acute onset + toxic + 4 Ds (Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia, Distress) + tripod + NO cough, in an under-Hib-immunized child. Distinguish from croup (gradual, barky cough, non-toxic). RN role: rapid recognition + keep calm/upright + do NOT examine the throat + immediate escalation/team activation.

labs.n: Protecting the airway — what NOT to do + keeping the child calm
labs.v: THE CARDINAL PRINCIPLE in suspected epiglottitis is to do NOTHING that could agitate the child or instrument the airway, because crying, gagging, or oropharyngeal manipulation can trigger laryngospasm and precipitate COMPLETE, FATAL AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION. Therefore, until a definitive airway is secured, DO NOT: (1) examine or visualize the throat / use a TONGUE DEPRESSOR / attempt to see the epiglottis at the bedside; (2) obtain a THROAT SWAB or culture; (3) start an IV or draw blood; (4) take a RECTAL temperature or perform other invasive/uncomfortable procedures; (5) force the child to LIE SUPINE (lying flat can worsen obstruction — keep upright); (6) SEPARATE the child from the parent or otherwise frighten him; (7) leave the child UNATTENDED. INSTEAD: keep the child CALM and UPRIGHT in a POSITION OF COMFORT (commonly sitting on the parent's lap, tripod/sniffing position), MINIMIZE stimulation (quiet room, limit personnel, dim unnecessary alarms), let the PARENT remain and help soothe him, offer BLOW-BY humidified OXYGEN held near the face ONLY if it does not cause distress (do not force a mask), and continuously but unobtrusively MONITOR (pulse oximetry, observation of work of breathing, color, mental status). IMAGING: a lateral neck X-ray can show the 'THUMBPRINT SIGN' (an edematous epiglottis), but it is obtained ONLY if the child is stable AND accompanied by personnel able to manage the airway — NEVER send an unstable child to radiology alone, and NEVER delay airway control for a film; if obstruction is impending, go straight to definitive airway management. All the deferred tasks (IV, labs, cultures, antibiotics, throat visualization) are performed AFTER the airway is secured (often during/after intubation in the OR). This restraint is counterintuitive but lifesaving: the safest immediate 'intervention' is calm observation + rapid mobilization of the airway team, not hands-on procedures.
labs.r: Do NOT agitate or instrument the airway: no throat exam/tongue depressor, no throat swab, no IV/blood draw, no rectal temp, do not force supine, do not separate from parent, never leave alone. DO keep calm + upright in a position of comfort with the parent, blow-by O2 if tolerated, continuous unobtrusive monitoring. Imaging only if stable + airway-trained escort; never delay the airway for a film. RN role: protect the airway by minimizing stimulation + deferring agitating tasks.

labs.n: Leadership, escalation, SBAR, and role delegation in the emergency
labs.v: Survival in epiglottitis depends on the nurse rapidly ORCHESTRATING the right people and equipment — this is fundamentally a LEADERSHIP, DELEGATION, and COMMUNICATION challenge: (1) PRIORITIZE by ABCs — the threatened AIRWAY is the single highest priority; everything else (IV, labs, antipyretics) waits. (2) ESCALATE immediately through the CHAIN OF COMMAND — do NOT attempt to manage alone or delay: notify the emergency provider STAT and ACTIVATE the EMERGENCY AIRWAY TEAM — ENT (otolaryngology) and ANESTHESIA (the providers who will secure the airway), plus a rapid-response/code resource, RESPIRATORY THERAPY, and alert the OPERATING ROOM and PICU. Use the facility's escalation pathway; if a needed responder is delayed, escalate up the chain. (3) Communicate with SBAR for a concise, complete handoff to each arriving responder — SITUATION (4-year-old, suspected epiglottitis, impending airway obstruction), BACKGROUND (acute fever, drooling, muffled voice, tripod, under-immunized for Hib), ASSESSMENT (toxic, stridor, SpO2, work of breathing, kept calm/upright, throat not examined), RECOMMENDATION (need a controlled airway in the OR now; ENT + anesthesia + emergency tracheostomy backup). (4) Use CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION (give a clear order to a named person; they repeat it back; confirm completion) and assign explicit ROLES as the team assembles: one person brings the DIFFICULT-AIRWAY/INTUBATION CART + EMERGENCY TRACHEOSTOMY TRAY + BAG-VALVE-MASK; one prepares medications + IV access for AFTER the airway is secured; one continuously MONITORS the child; one STAYS WITH AND SUPPORTS THE PARENT (and the child's calm); one DOCUMENTS events + times; the most experienced airway providers lead the intubation. (5) DELEGATE APPROPRIATELY within scope: UNLICENSED ASSISTIVE PERSONNEL (UAP) can FETCH equipment, bring the airway cart, gather supplies, run for help, and assist in keeping the environment calm — but the RN does NOT delegate the AIRWAY ASSESSMENT, the ongoing monitoring/clinical judgment of a deteriorating patient, or patient teaching; an LPN/LVN can perform within-scope tasks but the RN retains assessment + coordination; the RN stays with the child. (6) Do NOT delegate or perform agitating/non-essential tasks now (the deferred IV/labs/throat exam). (7) After the airway is secured and the child is stabilized, DEBRIEF the team (what went well, opportunities) and support staff. THIS CASE: the RN recognized the emergency, kept the child calm/upright with mother, escalated + activated ENT/anesthesia/PICU/RT + readied the OR, gave SBAR, assigned roles with closed-loop communication, and deferred all agitating tasks — leading to a controlled OR intubation without complications.
labs.r: Leadership/delegation in epiglottitis: prioritize AIRWAY (ABCs); ESCALATE via chain of command + activate the airway team (ENT + anesthesia + RT + PICU + OR); SBAR handoffs; CLOSED-LOOP communication + explicit role assignment (airway cart/trach tray/BVM, meds/IV for after, monitor, parent support, documentation); delegate equipment-fetching/comfort to UAP but NEVER the airway assessment/monitoring/judgment; defer agitating tasks; debrief after. RN role: orchestrate people + equipment while keeping the child calm.

labs.n: Definitive airway + antibiotics + droplet precautions + prevention
labs.v: DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT = SECURE THE AIRWAY in a CONTROLLED setting BEFORE complete obstruction: the most experienced airway providers (ENT + ANESTHESIA) perform INTUBATION in the OPERATING ROOM under INHALATIONAL (gas) anesthesia, keeping the child breathing spontaneously, with EMERGENCY TRACHEOSTOMY (and cricothyrotomy) equipment and a surgeon immediately ready in case intubation fails ('can't intubate, can't ventilate'). Bag-valve-mask ventilation can usually oxygenate temporarily if needed. A small endotracheal tube is used because of the swelling. AFTER the airway is secured: obtain IV access + BLOOD CULTURES, then start IV ANTIBIOTICS — typically a third-generation cephalosporin (CEFTRIAXONE or cefotaxime), with VANCOMYCIN added if MRSA/resistant organisms are a concern per local protocol; provide humidified oxygen, sedation/comfort while intubated, and PICU monitoring; corticosteroids may be used to reduce airway edema. The child is extubated when the epiglottic swelling resolves (often 24-72 hours; confirmed by an air leak around the tube and/or improved appearance) and can protect the airway. DROPLET PRECAUTIONS are instituted (Hib + other respiratory organisms spread by droplets). PREVENTION + PUBLIC HEALTH: the HIB CONJUGATE VACCINE has made Hib epiglottitis rare — this child's INCOMPLETE Hib immunization is a key risk factor, so CATCH-UP immunization is arranged during recovery; for confirmed invasive Hib disease, RIFAMPIN PROPHYLAXIS is recommended for certain UNIMMUNIZED/under-immunized close/household contacts (e.g., the infant sibling) per public-health guidance, and the case may be reportable. EXPECTED course with timely airway control + antibiotics: rapid improvement, extubation within days, and full recovery. THIS CHILD: intubated in the OR by anesthesia/ENT (cherry-red swollen epiglottis seen), emergency trach backup ready but not needed; IV ceftriaxone started after the airway was secured; droplet precautions; extubated hospital day 2 as edema resolved; catch-up Hib + contact prophylaxis addressed.
labs.r: Definitive care = controlled OR intubation by ENT + anesthesia under inhalational anesthesia with emergency tracheostomy backup; THEN IV access + blood cultures + IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime ± vancomycin) + humidified O2 + PICU; extubate when edema resolves (air leak). Droplet precautions. Prevent with Hib vaccine (this child under-immunized → catch-up) + rifampin prophylaxis for under-immunized close contacts in Hib disease. RN role: facilitate the controlled airway + post-airway treatment + precautions + prevention.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct, lifesaving practice + reassuring findings): the throat was NOT examined and NO tongue depressor/throat swab was used; NO IV/blood draw or rectal temperature was attempted before the airway was secured; the child was NOT forced to lie supine and was NOT separated from his mother; he was NOT left unattended and was NOT sent to radiology for a neck film while unstable; airway management was NOT delayed for imaging or labs; this is NOT croup (no barky cough, toxic + drooling + muffled voice + acute onset = epiglottitis, not viral laryngotracheobronchitis — so nebulized epinephrine/steroids-and-discharge would be inappropriate as the primary plan); NO complete airway obstruction occurred (the controlled airway was secured in time); NO failed intubation requiring emergency surgical airway (tracheostomy backup ready but not needed); NO anaphylaxis/foreign-body aspiration (history + presentation fit infection); the RN did NOT attempt to manage the airway emergency alone or delay escalation, and did NOT delegate the airway assessment/monitoring to unlicensed staff. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: immediate triage RECOGNITION of the airway threat + a calm, upright, parent-held approach; rapid ESCALATION + activation of the right team (ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT + OR) with SBAR + closed-loop communication + clear role assignment; a CONTROLLED OR intubation by the most experienced providers with surgical-airway backup; prompt post-airway antibiotics + PICU monitoring; droplet precautions; an engaged, cooperative parent able to keep the child calm; interpreter availability; and arranged catch-up Hib immunization + contact prophylaxis to prevent recurrence/spread. EXPECTED trajectory: airway secured without obstruction, rapid response to antibiotics, extubation within days, and full recovery — a result driven as much by what the team WISELY DID NOT DO (no agitation/throat exam) and by EFFECTIVE LEADERSHIP/COORDINATION as by any single procedure.
labs.r: Negatives confirm correct practice: no throat exam/tongue depressor/swab, no IV/rectal temp/supine/separation before the airway is secured, no delay for imaging, not treated as croup, no obstruction or failed airway, and the RN escalated + did not delegate the airway assessment. Protective: rapid recognition + calm parent-held approach + escalation/team activation + controlled OR airway + antibiotics + prevention. Outcome driven by restraint + leadership. RN role: protect the airway + lead the team.

nursesNote: ED, evening — 4 y/o boy L. Castillo brought by parents with ABRUPT (~6 hr) high fever, refusal to eat/drink, DROOLING, muffled voice, and worsening breathing. On arrival: TOXIC-appearing, anxious, sitting bolt upright in TRIPOD position on mother's lap, DROOLING, muffled 'hot-potato' voice, soft inspiratory STRIDOR + mild retractions, NO cough. VS: HR 150, BP 98/60, RR 34 with stridor, SpO2 93% RA (→94% on tolerated blow-by humidified O2), T 39.4°C. Constellation (acute + toxic + 4 Ds: Drooling/Dysphagia/Dysphonia/Distress + tripod + no cough) = classic ACUTE EPIGLOTTITIS — airway emergency. Immunizations INCOMPLETE (missed Hib series). Lives with parents + infant sibling; mother at bedside, frightened but cooperative; bilingual home (interpreter available). TRIAGE/RN actions: recognized the airway threat immediately; took child + mother straight to a resuscitation room; kept him CALM + UPRIGHT on mother's lap; did NOT examine the throat / no tongue depressor / no throat swab; did NOT start an IV, draw blood, take a rectal temp, or force him supine; did NOT separate him from mother; did NOT leave him alone; offered blow-by humidified O2 (tolerated). ESCALATION + LEADERSHIP (+5 min): notified provider STAT + activated EMERGENCY AIRWAY TEAM — ENT + ANESTHESIA + PICU + RESPIRATORY THERAPY paged STAT + OR notified/readied; gave SBAR handoff (S: 4 y/o, suspected epiglottitis, impending obstruction; B: acute fever/drooling/muffled voice/tripod, under-immunized for Hib; A: toxic, stridor, SpO2 94% on blow-by, kept calm/upright, throat not examined; R: controlled airway in OR now, ENT+anesthesia, trach backup). Brought DIFFICULT-AIRWAY/INTUBATION CART + EMERGENCY TRACHEOSTOMY TRAY + BVM to bedside; assigned ROLES with closed-loop communication (airway equipment; meds + IV access prepared for AFTER airway; continuous monitoring; parent support; documentation/timing); DEFERRED IV/labs/throat exam/antibiotics until airway secured; UAP delegated to fetch equipment + help keep environment calm (RN retained airway assessment + monitoring). +25 min: airway SECURED in the OR by anesthesia/ENT under inhalational anesthesia (cherry-red, swollen epiglottis visualized); small ETT placed; emergency surgical-airway backup ready but not needed; then IV access obtained + blood cultures drawn + IV ceftriaxone (± vancomycin per protocol) started; transferred to PICU on droplet precautions. PICU hr 4: defervescing (38.2), sedated/comfortable on ventilator, parents updated + at bedside, monitoring airway edema for resolution. Day 2: epiglottic edema resolved (air leak + improved appearance) → successfully EXTUBATED; breathing comfortably without stridor; tolerating oral fluids; continuing IV antibiotics; calm + interactive with parents. Day 4: afebrile + normal breathing + eating/playful; transitioning to oral antibiotics to complete course; CATCH-UP IMMUNIZATIONS (incl. Hib) arranged; household-contact rifampin prophylaxis addressed with public health (infant sibling) if Hib confirmed; discharge teaching + follow-up scheduled. Team DEBRIEF completed. Outcome driven by what was WISELY NOT done (no agitation/throat exam) + effective leadership/coordination of the airway team.
providerOrders: ACTIVATE EMERGENCY AIRWAY TEAM immediately: ENT (otolaryngology) + anesthesia + PICU + respiratory therapy; notify/ready the OR for a controlled airway
DIAGNOSIS: acute epiglottitis (supraglottitis) with impending airway obstruction in an under-Hib-immunized 4-year-old
CODE STATUS: full code; parents present/decision-makers
AIRWAY PROTECTION (do NOT agitate — until airway secured):
Keep the child CALM and UPRIGHT in a position of comfort (on the parent's lap); minimize stimulation; limit personnel; keep the parent at the bedside
DO NOT examine/visualize the throat, use a tongue depressor, or obtain a throat swab/culture
DO NOT start an IV, draw blood, give oral/rectal meds, take a rectal temperature, or force the child supine
DO NOT leave the child unattended; do NOT send for a neck X-ray unless stable AND escorted by airway-trained staff (never delay the airway for imaging)
Provide blow-by humidified oxygen only if tolerated without distress; continuous SpO2 + observation of work of breathing/color/mental status
Keep difficult-airway/intubation cart + emergency tracheostomy tray + bag-valve-mask at the bedside
LEADERSHIP / ESCALATION / COMMUNICATION:
Prioritize by ABCs (airway first); escalate via chain of command without delay
SBAR handoff to each responder; closed-loop communication; assign explicit roles (airway equipment, meds/IV for after, monitoring, parent support, documentation/timing)
Delegate equipment-fetching/comfort tasks to UAP within scope; RN retains airway assessment, monitoring, and clinical judgment; RN stays with the child
DEFINITIVE AIRWAY (controlled):
Intubation in the OR by the most experienced providers (ENT/anesthesia) under inhalational anesthesia; emergency tracheostomy/cricothyrotomy backup ready
Use an appropriately small ETT for the swelling; confirm placement; secure
AFTER AIRWAY SECURED:
Obtain IV access + blood cultures; start IV antibiotics — ceftriaxone or cefotaxime (± vancomycin per protocol)
Humidified oxygen; sedation/comfort while intubated; PICU admission + monitoring
Consider corticosteroids to reduce airway edema per team
Plan extubation when epiglottic edema resolves (air leak / improved exam), typically 24-72 h
INFECTION CONTROL + PREVENTION:
Droplet precautions
Arrange catch-up immunizations including Hib during recovery
For confirmed invasive Hib: rifampin prophylaxis for under-immunized close/household contacts (e.g., infant sibling) per public health; report per requirements
FAMILY + SUPPORT:
Keep parent present to maintain the child's calm; update parents frequently; professional interpreter as needed; child-life support post-stabilization
Team DEBRIEF after stabilization; staff support
DISCHARGE PLANNING:
Complete the antibiotic course (IV → oral); confirm airway/feeding back to baseline
Immunization catch-up plan + primary care follow-up; teaching on completing the vaccine series + when to seek emergency care

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this airway emergency?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Classic epiglottitis presentation — acute onset, high fever, toxic appearance, the 4 Ds (Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia/muffled 'hot-potato' voice, Distress), tripod positioning, inspiratory stridor, and NO cough
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing epiglottitis (a life-threatening airway emergency) drives every subsequent action. RN role: rapid recognition + escalation.

opts.text: A threatened airway with stridor + retractions + SpO2 93% — AIRWAY is the highest priority (ABCs); impending obstruction can occur within hours
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the airway threat is the top priority. RN role: prioritize airway + mobilize definitive airway management.

opts.text: Do NOT examine the throat / use a tongue depressor / swab — and keep the child calm and upright with the parent — because agitation or oropharyngeal stimulation can precipitate complete obstruction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cardinal 'do not agitate/examine' rule prevents fatal obstruction. RN role: protect the airway by minimizing stimulation.

opts.text: Immediate escalation + activation of the emergency airway team (ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT + OR) through the chain of command — this is not a one-nurse problem
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — survival depends on rapid escalation + the right team. RN role: escalate + activate the team without delay.

opts.text: Use SBAR + closed-loop communication and assign clear roles (airway cart/trach tray/BVM, meds/IV for after, monitoring, parent support, documentation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — effective leadership/communication coordinates the response. RN role: SBAR + closed-loop communication + role assignment.

opts.text: Defer agitating/non-essential tasks (IV, blood draw, labs, antibiotics, throat exam) until the airway is secured
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — deferring agitating tasks protects the airway. RN role: prioritize the airway + delay stimulation.

opts.text: Incomplete Hib immunization is a key risk factor and informs prevention (catch-up vaccine, contact prophylaxis)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — under-immunization explains susceptibility + drives prevention. RN role: note immunization status + plan catch-up/prophylaxis.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant for antibiotics given after the airway is secured. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Use a tongue depressor to visualize the epiglottis and obtain a throat culture to confirm the diagnosis before calling anyone
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Examining/instrumenting the throat can trigger laryngospasm and complete fatal obstruction. Do NOT examine the throat; diagnosis is clinical, and the airway team is activated immediately — visualization happens only in a controlled setting (OR) by airway providers.

opts.text: Lay the child flat, start an IV for fluids and antibiotics right away, and give a nebulized treatment to calm the stridor
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Forcing the child supine can worsen obstruction; an IV/agitating procedures can precipitate obstruction and must be deferred until the airway is secured; and this is epiglottitis (not croup), so croup-style nebulized treatment is not the primary management. Keep upright/calm + secure the airway first.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What principles apply to this emergency?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Epiglottitis is a rapidly progressive bacterial supraglottic infection (classically Hib; now also S. pneumoniae/GAS/S. aureus, especially in under-immunized children) that can obstruct the airway within hours
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology + the time-critical airway threat. Foundation for urgency.

opts.text: Agitation or oropharyngeal instrumentation can precipitate laryngospasm and complete obstruction → keep the child calm/upright, do not examine the throat, and defer agitating tasks until the airway is secured
opts.rat: CORRECT — the do-not-agitate principle. Foundation for airway protection.

opts.text: Epiglottitis (acute, toxic, drooling, muffled voice, NO cough) is distinct from croup (gradual, barky cough, hoarse, non-toxic) — so management differs (controlled airway vs nebulized epinephrine/steroids)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the epiglottitis-vs-croup distinction. Foundation for correct management.

opts.text: Definitive management is a CONTROLLED airway in the OR by ENT/anesthesia under inhalational anesthesia with emergency tracheostomy backup; antibiotics + cultures come AFTER the airway is secured
opts.rat: CORRECT — definitive airway-first sequence. Foundation for the management plan.

opts.text: Leadership in the emergency = prioritize airway (ABCs), escalate via chain of command, activate the airway team, use SBAR + closed-loop communication, and assign clear roles
opts.rat: CORRECT — leadership/communication framework. Foundation for coordinating the response.

opts.text: Delegation principle: UAP may fetch equipment/supplies and help keep the environment calm, but the RN does NOT delegate airway assessment, monitoring of a deteriorating patient, or clinical judgment
opts.rat: CORRECT — scope/delegation principle. Foundation for safe task assignment.

opts.text: Because the child is in respiratory distress, the nurse should immediately attempt bedside intubation alone to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The airway must be secured in a CONTROLLED setting by the most experienced providers (ENT/anesthesia, ideally in the OR) with surgical-airway backup; an unprepared bedside attempt can precipitate complete obstruction. The nurse escalates + coordinates, not solo-intubates.

opts.text: A normal-appearing throat on inspection would rule out epiglottitis, so a quick look is worthwhile to be sure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The throat must NOT be inspected (risk of precipitating obstruction); the diagnosis is clinical, and visualization is done only in a controlled OR setting by airway providers.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Protect the threatened AIRWAY r/t impending obstruction — keep the child calm/upright with the parent, do NOT examine the throat or agitate him, give blow-by O2 if tolerated, and monitor continuously
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway (ABCs) is the immediate life threat; avoiding agitation prevents complete obstruction. RN role: protect the airway.

opts.text: Escalate + mobilize the definitive airway team r/t the need for a controlled airway — activate ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT + OR via the chain of command
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Definitive airway control requires the right team fast. RN role: escalate + activate the team.

opts.text: Coordinate the response r/t multiple responders/tasks — SBAR handoffs, closed-loop communication, and clear role assignment with the airway cart/trach tray/BVM ready
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Effective coordination turns a team into a functioning response. RN role: SBAR + role assignment + equipment readiness.

opts.text: Defer + then perform post-airway care r/t treatment needs — after the airway is secured, obtain IV access + blood cultures + start IV antibiotics + PICU monitoring + droplet precautions
opts.rat: #4 — Important but sequenced AFTER the airway. RN role: deliver post-airway treatment + precautions.

opts.text: Family support + prevention r/t the frightened parent/child and under-immunization — keep the parent present to calm the child, update them, and arrange catch-up Hib + contact prophylaxis
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for prevention; parental presence also aids airway protection (calm). RN role: family support + prevention planning.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this child with suspected epiglottitis.

opts.text: Keep the child calm and upright on the parent's lap, minimize stimulation, and provide blow-by humidified oxygen if tolerated
opts.rat: Protects the airway by avoiding agitation + supports oxygenation. RN role: calm, upright, minimal-stimulation care.

opts.text: Immediately escalate and activate the emergency airway team (ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT) and ready the OR for a controlled airway
opts.rat: Definitive airway control requires the team urgently. RN role: escalate + activate.

opts.text: Give an SBAR handoff, use closed-loop communication, assign roles, and bring the difficult-airway cart + emergency tracheostomy tray + BVM to the bedside
opts.rat: Coordinates the response + readies equipment. RN role: communication + role assignment + equipment.

opts.text: Defer the IV, blood draws, labs, antibiotics, and throat exam until the airway is secured
opts.rat: Avoids agitation that could precipitate obstruction. RN role: prioritize airway + delay stimulation.

opts.text: After the airway is secured, obtain IV access + blood cultures and start IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime ± vancomycin), admit to PICU, and institute droplet precautions
opts.rat: Correct post-airway treatment + precautions. RN role: deliver treatment after the airway is controlled.

opts.text: Delegate fetching the airway cart/supplies and helping keep the environment calm to UAP, while the RN retains airway assessment and monitoring
opts.rat: Appropriate delegation within scope. RN role: delegate equipment/comfort tasks; retain assessment/monitoring.

opts.text: Arrange catch-up Hib immunization and address rifampin prophylaxis for under-immunized close contacts (per public health) once stabilized
opts.rat: Prevention + public-health follow-up. RN role: immunization catch-up + contact prophylaxis planning.

opts.text: Use a tongue depressor to inspect the throat and obtain a throat swab to confirm the diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — throat inspection/instrumentation can precipitate complete obstruction. Diagnosis is clinical; visualization only in a controlled OR setting.

opts.text: Lay the child flat, restrain him for an IV, and send him to radiology alone for a neck X-ray
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — supine positioning + restraint/IV agitation can worsen/precipitate obstruction, and an unstable child must never go to radiology unescorted; never delay the airway for a film.

opts.text: Manage it as croup with nebulized epinephrine and oral steroids, then plan to discharge home
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — this is epiglottitis (toxic, drooling, muffled voice, no cough), an airway emergency requiring a controlled airway + IV antibiotics, not croup management/discharge.

opts.text: Have the bedside nurse attempt intubation alone immediately to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the airway must be secured by the most experienced providers (ENT/anesthesia) in a controlled setting with surgical backup; an unprepared attempt risks precipitating complete obstruction.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Keep the child calm and upright with the parent, avoid throat exam/agitation, give blow-by O2 if tolerated, and stay with him while monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediately protect the airway by preventing agitation/obstruction. RN role: calm/upright/monitor.

opts.text: Escalate via the chain of command and activate the emergency airway team (ENT + anesthesia + PICU + RT) and ready the OR
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Mobilize definitive airway management without delay. RN role: escalate + activate.

opts.text: Give SBAR, assign roles with closed-loop communication, and bring the difficult-airway cart + tracheostomy tray + BVM to the bedside
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Coordinate the responders + ready the equipment. RN role: communication + roles + equipment.

opts.text: Support the controlled airway in the OR, then obtain IV access + blood cultures + start IV antibiotics and admit to PICU on droplet precautions
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Secure the airway, then deliver post-airway treatment. RN role: facilitate airway + post-airway care.

opts.text: Update/support the family, then plan catch-up immunization + contact prophylaxis and debrief the team
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/after. Family support, prevention, and team debrief. RN role: support + prevention + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The RN kept the child calm/upright with mother, did not examine the throat, escalated immediately, gave SBAR, assigned roles, and readied the airway cart/trach tray — and the airway was secured in the OR without obstruction
opts.rat: Correct airway protection + leadership/coordination led to a controlled airway without complication. Model handling/outcome.

opts.text: After a controlled OR intubation by ENT/anesthesia, the child was admitted to PICU on IV ceftriaxone with droplet precautions, defervescing and comfortable
opts.rat: Definitive airway + post-airway antibiotics + precautions = expected positive course. Foundation for recovery.

opts.text: Hospital day 2: epiglottic edema resolved (air leak present) → successfully extubated, breathing without stridor, tolerating oral fluids on continued antibiotics
opts.rat: Resolution of edema + successful extubation = recovery. Expected trajectory with timely treatment.

opts.text: By day 4: afebrile, breathing normally, eating and playful; transitioning to oral antibiotics with catch-up Hib immunization + contact prophylaxis arranged
opts.rat: Full recovery + prevention planning. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a clinician used a tongue depressor to inspect the throat; the child immediately developed laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction with desaturation, requiring emergency airway rescue
opts.rat: ⚠️ Throat instrumentation precipitated complete obstruction — the catastrophic outcome the do-not-examine rule prevents. Demonstrates why the throat is never examined outside a controlled setting.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse laid the child flat and restrained him to start an IV; he became more agitated and his stridor worsened with rising work of breathing and falling SpO2
opts.rat: ⚠️ Supine positioning + agitating procedures worsened the obstruction. Demonstrates why agitating tasks are deferred and the child is kept calm/upright.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the unstable child was sent to radiology alone for a neck film, delaying airway management; he obstructed in the hallway away from the airway team
opts.rat: ⚠️ Sending an unstable child for imaging unescorted + delaying the airway led to obstruction without the team present. Demonstrates never delaying the airway for a film/never leaving the child unescorted.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the case was treated as croup with nebulized epinephrine and discharged; the child returned in extremis with airway obstruction
opts.rat: ⚠️ Misdiagnosing epiglottitis as croup and discharging missed a true airway emergency. Demonstrates the importance of distinguishing the two.

opts.text: While awaiting the airway team, the child remained calm and upright on mother's lap with stable SpO2 on blow-by O2 and unchanged (not worsening) work of breathing under close monitoring
opts.rat: Maintaining a calm, upright, monitored state holds the airway stable while the team mobilizes — neither improved nor worsened yet, which is the goal until definitive airway control.

opts.text: One week later: fully recovered, completing antibiotics, immunization series being caught up, and household contact prophylaxis completed per public health
opts.rat: Complete recovery + prevention closure. Standard ideal longer-term outcome.
